title: Double-fan adhesive binding
subtitle: PVA applied in two fan passes to produce a flexible, lay-flat spine without sewing
excerpt: Double-fan binding is a commercial technique adapted for the studio. Two passes of PVA, applied while the text block is fanned alternately in each direction, penetrate slightly between every leaf. The result lies flat when open with no sewing marks showing on the spine.
sourceNotes: Double-fan adhesive binding is documented in contemporary bookbinding practice literature including Arthur W. Johnson, The Thames and Hudson Manual of Bookbinding (1978). Post-1928 and copyright; treated as synthesised craft knowledge without direct quotation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A method of applying adhesive to a text block by fanning the pages in alternating directions so glue enters every leaf joint.
A bookbinding method where the spine is ground or nipped to allow glue to penetrate between pages. Double-fan is a refinement that achieves similar penetration without grinding.

content.text: Standard
perfect bound
books have their spine ground or roughed before gluing, which shortens the pages. The
fan binding

method achieves similar glue penetration by fanning the text block so each leaf joint opens slightly at the spine edge. Two passes in opposite directions means every joint receives adhesive on both faces.
Acid-free PVA is safe for indoor use. Clean the brush immediately after use. PVA dried on a brush is very difficult to remove.
What you need
Text block: loose sheets of 80 gsm or heavier paper, jogged square and even at the head and spine. The sheets must be dry and straight; any curl will prevent even fanning.
PVA: medium consistency, not too runny. Runny PVA wicks between pages further than the spine edge, creating stuck areas in the text.
The move
Jog the text block square at head and spine. Clamp between two pressing boards in the book press with the spine proud by 5 mm.

Fan the text block toward you by pressing the top edge away from you. Hold the fan at a consistent 15 degree lean. Brush PVA along the fanned spine in a single stroke, working the glue into the opened joints.
Release and jog the block back to square. Fan it in the opposite direction, pressing the top edge toward you. Brush PVA along the spine again.
Return the block to square immediately. Clamp in the press with the spine flush. Leave until the PVA is fully cured, at least 3 hours.
Apply a second coat of PVA to the flat spine surface and allow to tack. Attach a spine strip of mull or book cloth and continue to the cover.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Pages pull free when the book is opened wide
attrs.cause: The fan angle was too shallow and the glue did not penetrate far enough between the leaf joints.

content.text: Increase the fan angle to about 20 degrees. Also check that PVA was not too diluted; a slightly stiffer consistency penetrates the fan joints more reliably.

attrs.symptom: Two or more pages stuck together in the text block
attrs.cause: PVA was applied too liberally and wicked past the spine edge.

content.text: Use a stiffer PVA and a drier brush. Only the spine edge should receive glue, not the first centimetre of each page.
Where it leads

Double-fan binding works for single-leaf text blocks such as computer-printed or photocopied pages. It is the correct structure for a lay-flat notebook where sewing would be impractical. Pair it with a drum-leaf album structure for very heavy sheets that need both fanning and gluing.
